content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Users reported an optimization bug in LLVM. Maintainers provided a fix, but fixes often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original bug description and failing test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the fix)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

  1 //===- SLPVectorizer.cpp - A bottom up SLP Vectorizer ---------------------===//
  2 //
  3 // Part of the LLVM Project, under the Apache License v2.0 with LLVM Exceptions.
  4 // See https://llvm.org/LICENSE.txt for license information.
  5 // SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 WITH LLVM-exception
  6 //
  7 //===----------------------------------------------------------------------===//
  8 //
  9 // This pass implements the Bottom Up SLP vectorizer. It detects consecutive
 10 // stores that can be put together into vector-stores. Next, it attempts to
 11 // construct vectorizable tree using the use-def chains. If a profitable tree
 12 // was found, the SLP vectorizer performs vectorization on the tree.
 13 //
 14 // The pass is inspired by the work described in the paper:
 15 //  "Loop-Aware SLP in GCC" by Ira Rosen, Dorit Nuzman, Ayal Zaks.
 16 //

150 /// Limits the size of scheduling regions in a block.
151 /// It avoid long compile times for _very_ large blocks where vector
152 /// instructions are spread over a wide range.
153 /// This limit is way higher than needed by real-world functions.
154 static cl::opt<int>

171 // The higher this value, the higher the compilation time overhead.
172 static cl::opt<int> LookAheadMaxDepth(
173     "slp-max-look-ahead-depth", cl::init(2), cl::Hidden,
174     cl::desc("The maximum look-ahead depth for operand reordering scores"));

177 // when it probing among candidates for vectorization tree roots.
178 // The higher this value, the higher the compilation time overhead but unlike
179 // similar limit for operands ordering this is less frequently used, hence

181 static cl::opt<int> RootLookAheadMaxDepth(
182     "slp-max-root-look-ahead-depth", cl::init(2), cl::Hidden,
183     cl::desc("The maximum look-ahead depth for searching best rooting option"));

202 // Limit the number of alias checks. The limit is chosen so that
203 // it has no negative effect on the llvm benchmarks.
204 static const unsigned AliasedCheckLimit = 10;
205 
206 // Limit of the number of uses for potentially transformed instructions/values,
207 // used in checks to avoid compile-time explode.
208 static constexpr int UsesLimit = 8;
209 
210 // Another limit for the alias checks: The maximum distance between load/store
211 // instructions where alias checks are done.
212 // This limit is useful for very large basic blocks.
213 static const unsigned MaxMemDepDistance = 160;
214 
215 /// If the ScheduleRegionSizeBudget is exhausted, we allow small scheduling
216 /// regions to be handled.
217 static const int MinScheduleRegionSize = 16;
218 
219 /// Maximum allowed number of operands in the PHI nodes.
220 static const unsigned MaxPHINumOperands = 128;
221 
222 /// Predicate for the element types that the SLP vectorizer supports.
223 ///
224 /// The most important thing to filter here are types which are invalid in LLVM
225 /// vectors. We also filter target specific types which have absolutely no
226 /// meaningful vectorization path such as x86_fp80 and ppc_f128. This just
227 /// avoids spending time checking the cost model and realizing that they will
228 /// be inevitably scalarized.
229 static bool isValidElementType(Type *Ty) {
230   return VectorType::isValidElementType(Ty) && !Ty->isX86_FP80Ty() &&
231          !Ty->isPPC_FP128Ty();
232 }
233 
234 /// \returns True if the value is a constant (but not globals/constant
235 /// expressions).
236 static bool isConstant(Value *V) {
237   return isa<Constant>(V) && !isa<ConstantExpr, GlobalValue>(V);
238 }
239 
240 /// Checks if \p V is one of vector-like instructions, i.e. undef,
241 /// insertelement/extractelement with constant indices for fixed vector type or
242 /// extractvalue instruction.
243 static bool isVectorLikeInstWithConstOps(Value *V) {
244   if (!isa<InsertElementInst, ExtractElementInst>(V) &&
245       !isa<ExtractValueInst, UndefValue>(V))
246     return false;
247   auto *I = dyn_cast<Instruction>(V);
248   if (!I || isa<ExtractValueInst>(I))
249     return true;
250   if (!isa<FixedVectorType>(I->getOperand(0)->getType()))

name: stop
arguments:
{"thoughts": "## 1. Fix Understanding

The patch introduces a new method `removeInstructionsAndOperands` in the SLPVectorizer to handle instruction deletion more aggressively. The key changes are:

1. **New method `removeInstructionsAndOperands`**: This method not only marks instructions for deletion but also clears their operands and recursively marks trivially dead operands for deletion. This enables more vectorizable patterns and generates fewer useless extractelement instructions.

2. **Replaces `eraseInstruction` calls**: In `vectorizeTree()` and horizontal reduction handling, calls to `eraseInstruction` are replaced with `removeInstructionsAndOperands` to clean up dead operands.

3. **Adds `isDeleted` checks**: Throughout the code, checks for `R.isDeleted(I)` are added to skip already-deleted instructions when processing candidate lists for vectorization.

4. **Fixes in `~BoUpSLP()`**: Adds handling for instructions without parents (inserts them before terminator) before processing.

5. **Adds `isSignedMinBitwidthRootNode()`**: Helper method for reduction type casting.

## 2. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified

### Assumption 1: Single-user operand deletion safety
The code assumes that if an operand has only one user (the instruction being deleted) and is trivially dead, it's safe to delete it. However, this assumes the operand isn't part of the vectorization tree (`Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI` check).

### Assumption 2: Instruction parent existence
The code assumes instructions have parents when removing them, but handles the case where they don't in the destructor.

### Assumption 3: Use emptiness after deletion
The code asserts that instructions being deleted have no users or all users are also marked for deletion. This assumes proper tracking of the deletion state.

### Assumption 4: Trivial deadness determination
Relies on `wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead` and `isInstructionTriviallyDead` which have their own assumptions about instruction semantics.

### Assumption 5: Tree entry consistency
Assumes that if an instruction has a tree entry, its `VectorizedValue` field is properly set and represents the vectorized version.

### Assumption 6: Reduction reference clearing
Assumes that replacing uses with poison in reduction contexts is safe when `UserIgnoreList` contains the users.

## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

### Case 1: Circular dependencies in operand chain
If operand A uses operand B, and B uses A (through phi nodes or other constructs), the recursive deletion could get confused about which instruction is "trivially dead" first.

### Case 2: Instructions with side effects
Instructions with side effects (volatile loads/stores, atomic operations, calls with side effects) might be incorrectly considered "trivially dead" even with single users.

### Case 3: Instructions in multiple tree entries
If an instruction appears in multiple tree entries (via `MultiNodeScalars`), the check `Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI` might not catch all cases where the operand shouldn't be deleted.

### Case 4: Instructions with metadata or debug info
The salvageDebugInfo calls might not handle all debug info scenarios correctly when operands are being nulled out.

### Case 5: Instructions with poison/undef propagation
Replacing uses with poison in reduction contexts might change poison propagation semantics if not all users are in the `UserIgnoreList`.

### Case 6: Instructions with exception handling
Instructions in exception handling regions might have special constraints on deletion.

### Case 7: Instructions with lifetime markers
Instructions with `llvm.lifetime.start/end` intrinsics might have constraints on when they can be deleted.

### Case 8: Instructions in different basic blocks
The algorithm assumes all instructions are in the same function/context, but cross-block dependencies might exist.

### Case 9: Instructions with uses by non-instructions
The code assumes all users are instructions, but values could be used by constants, metadata, etc.

### Case 10: Race conditions in multi-pass processing
Since instructions are marked for deletion but not immediately erased, other parts of the vectorizer might see inconsistent state.", "strategies": [{"name": "Circular dependency chain", "target": "Create circular use-def chains between instructions being vectorized", "rationale": "The recursive deletion logic might not handle circular dependencies correctly, potentially deleting instructions that are still needed or causing infinite loops.", "expected_issue": "Incorrect deletion of live instructions or compiler crash due to infinite recursion."}, {"name": "Side-effect instruction operand", "target": "Include instructions with side effects (volatile, atomic, calls) as operands to vectorizable instructions", "rationale": "wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead might incorrectly mark side-effecting instructions as dead when they have single users.", "expected_issue": "Premature deletion of side-effecting instructions, changing program semantics."}, {"name": "Multi-tree-entry instruction", "target": "Create instructions that appear in multiple tree entries through MultiNodeScalars", "rationale": "The check Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI might miss cases where an operand appears in multiple entries but not as VectorizedValue.", "expected_issue": "Incorrect deletion of instructions still needed by other parts of the vectorization tree."}, {"name": "Poison propagation in reductions", "target": "Create reduction patterns where not all users are in UserIgnoreList", "rationale": "Replacing uses with poison might affect users outside the reduction tree, changing poison propagation semantics.", "expected_issue": "Incorrect poison propagation or miscompilation due to changed semantics."}, {"name": "Exception handling region", "target": "Place vectorizable instructions within exception handling regions", "rationale": "Instructions in EH regions might have special constraints on deletion that the algorithm doesn't consider.", "expected_issue": "Invalid IR after deletion or compiler crash."}, {"name": "Lifetime intrinsic users", "target": "Add llvm.lifetime.start/end intrinsics as users of vectorizable instructions", "rationale": "The deletion logic might not properly handle lifetime intrinsics, which have special semantics.", "expected_issue": "Incorrect memory lifetime analysis or compiler crash."}, {"name": "Cross-block dependencies", "target": "Create dependencies between instructions in different basic blocks", "rationale": "The algorithm assumes local processing but might miss cross-block dependencies when determining deadness.", "expected_issue": "Incorrect deletion of instructions with cross-block uses."}, {"name": "Metadata/dbg.value users", "target": "Add debug info metadata users to vectorizable instructions", "rationale": "salvageDebugInfo might not handle all debug info scenarios when operands are nulled out.", "expected_issue": "Debug info corruption or compiler crash."}, {"name": "Non-instruction users", "target": "Create uses of vectorizable instructions by non-instructions (constants, metadata, etc.)", "rationale": "The code assumes all users are instructions for deletion checks.", "expected_issue": "Assertion failure or incorrect deletion due to unhandled user types."}, {"name": "Instruction without parent during processing", "target": "Create scenario where instruction loses parent during vectorization process", "rationale": "The destructor handles missing parents, but other code might assume instructions have parents.", "expected_issue": "Null pointer dereference or incorrect behavior when removing instructions."}]}

      "Debug info corruption or compiler crash."
    ],
    [
      "Non-instruction users",
      "Create uses of vectorizable instructions by non-instructions (constants, metadata, etc.)",
      "The code assumes all users are instructions for deletion checks.",
      "Assertion failure or incorrect deletion due to unhandled user types."
    ],
    [
      "Instruction without parent during processing",
      "Create scenario where instruction loses parent during vectorization process",
      "The destructor handles missing parents, but other code might assume instructions have parents.",
      "Null pointer dereference or incorrect behavior when removing instructions."
    ]
  ],
  "thoughts": "## 1. Fix Understanding\n\nThe patch introduces a new method `removeInstructionsAndOperands` in the SLPVectorizer to handle instruction deletion more aggressively. The key changes are:\n\n1. **New method `removeInstructionsAndOperands`**: This method not only marks instructions for deletion but also clears their operands and recursively marks trivially dead operands for deletion. This enables more vectorizable patterns and generates fewer useless extractelement instructions.\n\n2. **Replaces `eraseInstruction` calls**: In `vectorizeTree()` and horizontal reduction handling, calls to `eraseInstruction` are replaced with `removeInstructionsAndOperands` to clean up dead operands.\n\n3. **Adds `isDeleted` checks**: Throughout the code, checks for `R.isDeleted(I)` are added to skip already-deleted instructions when processing candidate lists for vectorization.\n\n4. **Fixes in `~BoUpSLP()`**: Adds handling for instructions without parents (inserts them before terminator) before processing.\n\n5. **Adds `isSignedMinBitwidthRootNode()`**: Helper method for reduction type casting.\n\n## 2. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified\n\n### Assumption 1: Single-user operand deletion safety\nThe code assumes that if an operand has only one user (the instruction being deleted) and is trivially dead, it's safe to delete it. However, this assumes the operand isn't part of the vectorization tree (`Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI` check).\n\n### Assumption 2: Instruction parent existence\nThe code assumes instructions have parents when removing them, but handles the case where they don't in the destructor.\n\n### Assumption 3: Use emptiness after deletion\nThe code asserts that instructions being deleted have no users or all users are also marked for deletion. This assumes proper tracking of the deletion state.\n\n### Assumption 4: Trivial deadness determination\nRelies on `wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead` and `isInstructionTriviallyDead` which have their own assumptions about instruction semantics.\n\n### Assumption 5: Tree entry consistency\nAssumes that if an instruction has a tree entry, its `VectorizedValue` field is properly set and represents the vectorized version.\n\n### Assumption 6: Reduction reference clearing\nAssumes that replacing uses with poison in reduction contexts is safe when `UserIgnoreList` contains the users.\n\n## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions\n\n### Case 1: Circular dependencies in operand chain\nIf operand A uses operand B, and B uses A (through phi nodes or other constructs), the recursive deletion could get confused about which instruction is \"trivially dead\" first.\n\n### Case 2: Instructions with side effects\nInstructions with side effects (volatile loads/stores, atomic operations, calls with side effects) might be incorrectly considered \"trivially dead\" even with single users.\n\n### Case 3: Instructions in multiple tree entries\nIf an instruction appears in multiple tree entries (via `MultiNodeScalars`), the check `Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI` might not catch all cases where the operand shouldn't be deleted.\n\n### Case 4: Instructions with metadata or debug info\nThe salvageDebugInfo calls might not handle all debug info scenarios correctly when operands are being nulled out.\n\n### Case 5: Instructions with poison/undef propagation\nReplacing uses with poison in reduction contexts might change poison propagation semantics if not all users are in the `UserIgnoreList`.\n\n### Case 6: Instructions with exception handling\nInstructions in exception handling regions might have special constraints on deletion.\n\n### Case 7: Instructions with lifetime markers\nInstructions with `llvm.lifetime.start/end` intrinsics might have constraints on when they can be deleted.\n\n### Case 8: Instructions in different basic blocks\nThe algorithm assumes all instructions are in the same function/context, but cross-block dependencies might exist.\n\n### Case 9: Instructions with uses by non-instructions\nThe code assumes all users are instructions, but values could be used by constants, metadata, etc.\n\n### Case 10: Race conditions in multi-pass processing\nSince instructions are marked for deletion but not immediately erased, other parts of the vectorizer might see inconsistent state."
}

content:
# Phase 2: Generate Verified Test Cases

You have proposed the following test strategies in Phase 1:

[{'name': 'Circular dependency chain', 'target': 'Create circular use-def chains between instructions being vectorized', 'rationale': 'The recursive deletion logic might not handle circular dependencies correctly, potentially deleting instructions that are still needed or causing infinite loops.', 'expected_issue': 'Incorrect deletion of live instructions or compiler crash due to infinite recursion.'}, {'name': 'Side-effect instruction operand', 'target': 'Include instructions with side effects (volatile, atomic, calls) as operands to vectorizable instructions', 'rationale': 'wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead might incorrectly mark side-effecting instructions as dead when they have single users.', 'expected_issue': 'Premature deletion of side-effecting instructions, changing program semantics.'}, {'name': 'Multi-tree-entry instruction', 'target': 'Create instructions that appear in multiple tree entries through MultiNodeScalars', 'rationale': 'The check Entry->VectorizedValue == OpI might miss cases where an operand appears in multiple entries but not as VectorizedValue.', 'expected_issue': 'Incorrect deletion of instructions still needed by other parts of the vectorization tree.'}, {'name': 'Poison propagation in reductions', 'target': 'Create reduction patterns where not all users are in UserIgnoreList', 'rationale': 'Replacing uses with poison might affect users outside the reduction tree, changing poison propagation semantics.', 'expected_issue': 'Incorrect poison propagation or miscompilation due to changed semantics.'}, {'name': 'Exception handling region', 'target': 'Place vectorizable instructions within exception handling regions', 'rationale': "Instructions in EH regions might have special constraints on deletion that the algorithm doesn't consider.", 'expected_issue': 'Invalid IR after deletion or compiler crash.'}, {'name': 'Lifetime intrinsic users', 'target': 'Add llvm.lifetime.start/end intrinsics as users of vectorizable instructions', 'rationale': 'The deletion logic might not properly handle lifetime intrinsics, which have special semantics.', 'expected_issue': 'Incorrect memory lifetime analysis or compiler crash.'}, {'name': 'Cross-block dependencies', 'target': 'Create dependencies between instructions in different basic blocks', 'rationale': 'The algorithm assumes local processing but might miss cross-block dependencies when determining deadness.', 'expected_issue': 'Incorrect deletion of instructions with cross-block uses.'}, {'name': 'Metadata/dbg.value users', 'target': 'Add debug info metadata users to vectorizable instructions', 'rationale': 'salvageDebugInfo might not handle all debug info scenarios when operands are nulled out.', 'expected_issue': 'Debug info corruption or compiler crash.'}, {'name': 'Non-instruction users', 'target': 'Create uses of vectorizable instructions by non-instructions (constants, metadata, etc.)', 'rationale': 'The code assumes all users are instructions for deletion checks.', 'expected_issue': 'Assertion failure or incorrect deletion due to unhandled user types.'}, {'name': 'Instruction without parent during processing', 'target': 'Create scenario where instruction loses parent during vectorization process', 'rationale': 'The destructor handles missing parents, but other code might assume instructions have parents.', 'expected_issue': 'Null pointer dereference or incorrect behavior when removing instructions.'}]

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Select Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Select a test case that is most relevant to the issues you identified in Phase 1.

### Step 2: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 3: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 4: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

If the `verify` tool fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

name: tests_manager
arguments: {"action": "get", "index": 0}

output:
{
  "test_name": "<module>",
  "test_body": "; REQUIRES: x86-registered-target
; RUN: opt -passes=slp-vectorizer -S -o - %s \
; RUN: | FileCheck %s
; RUN: opt --try-experimental-debuginfo-iterators -passes=slp-vectorizer -S -o - %s \
; RUN: | FileCheck %s

;; $ cat test.cpp
;; float get();
;; float ext(float*);
;; void fun(float k1, float k2, float k3, float k4) {
;;   float quad[4];
;;   float c1 = get();
;;   float c2 = get();
;;   quad[0] = c1 - k1 - k3;
;;   quad[1] = c2 - k2 - k4;
;;   quad[2] = c1 - k1 + k3;
;;   quad[3] = c2 - k2 + k4;
;;   ext(quad);
;; }
;;
;; Generated by grabbingthe IR before SLP in:
;; $ clang++ -O2 -g test.cpp -Xclang -fexperimental-assignment-tracking

;; Test that dbg.assigns linked to the the scalar stores to quad get linked to
;; the vector store that replaces them.

; CHECK: #dbg_assign(float poison, ![[VAR:[0-9]+]], !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 0, 32), ![[ID:[0-9]+]], ptr %arrayidx, !DIExpression(),
; CHECK: #dbg_assign(float poison, ![[VAR]], !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 32, 32), ![[ID]], ptr %quad, !DIExpression(DW_OP_plus_uconst, 4),
; CHECK: #dbg_assign(float poison, ![[VAR]], !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 64, 32), ![[ID]], ptr %quad, !DIExpression(DW_OP_plus_uconst, 8),
; CHECK: store <4 x float> {{.*}} !DIAssignID ![[ID]]
; CHECK: #dbg_assign(float poison, ![[VAR]], !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 96, 32), ![[ID]], ptr %quad, !DIExpression(DW_OP_plus_uconst, 12),

target triple = "x86_64-unknown-unknown"

define dso_local void @_Z3funffff(float %k1, float %k2, float %k3, float %k4) local_unnamed_addr #0 !dbg !7 {
entry:
  %quad = alloca [4 x float], align 16, !DIAssignID !27
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata i1 undef, metadata !16, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !27, metadata ptr %quad, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %k1, metadata !12, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !30, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %k2, metadata !13, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !31, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %k3, metadata !14, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !32, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %k4, metadata !15, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !33, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  %call = tail call float @_Z3getv(), !dbg !35
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %call, metadata !20, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !36, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  %call1 = tail call float @_Z3getv(), !dbg !37
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %call1, metadata !21, metadata !DIExpression(), metadata !38, metadata ptr undef, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  %sub = fsub float %call, %k1, !dbg !39
  %sub2 = fsub float %sub, %k3, !dbg !40
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds [4 x float], ptr %quad, i64 0, i64 0, !dbg !41
  store float %sub2, ptr %arrayidx, align 16, !dbg !42, !DIAssignID !47
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %sub2, metadata !16, metadata !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 0, 32), metadata !47, metadata ptr %arrayidx, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  %sub3 = fsub float %call1, %k2, !dbg !48
  %sub4 = fsub float %sub3, %k4, !dbg !49
  %arrayidx5 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x float], ptr %quad, i64 0, i64 1, !dbg !50
  store float %sub4, ptr %arrayidx5, align 4, !dbg !51, !DIAssignID !52
  call void @llvm.dbg.assign(metadata float %sub4, metadata !16, metadata !DIExpression(DW_OP_LLVM_fragment, 32, 32), metadata !52, metadata ptr %arrayidx5, metadata !DIExpression()), !dbg !23
  %add = fadd float %sub, %k3, !dbg !53

  ret i32 0
}
```", "args": "-passes=slp-vectorizer", "thoughts": "Testing the "Non-instruction users" strategy. The test creates an add instruction %a that is used by a constant expression (getelementptr). When SLP vectorizes the stores, it should mark the add instructions for deletion via removeInstructionsAndOperands. The code assumes all users are instructions for deletion checks, but constant expressions are not instructions. This might cause issues when checking if the instruction has users or when determining if it's trivially dead."}

define dso_local i32 @test() {
entry:
  %a = add i32 1, 2
  %b = add i32 3, 4
  
  ; Create a circular dependency: %a uses %b, %b uses %a
  %c = add i32 %a, %b
  %d = add i32 %b, %a
  
  ; Store to array to enable vectorization
  %arr = alloca [4 x i32], align 16
  %gep0 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 0
  store i32 %a, ptr %gep0, align 4
  %gep1 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 1
  store i32 %b, ptr %gep1, align 4
  %gep2 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 2
  store i32 %c, ptr %gep2, align 4
  %gep3 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 3
  store i32 %d, ptr %gep3, align 4
  
  ret i32 0
}
```", "args": "-passes=slp-vectorizer", "thoughts": "Testing the "Circular dependency chain" strategy for test 2. The test creates a circular dependency where %c uses %a and %b, and %d uses %b and %a. When SLP vectorizes the stores, it should mark the add instructions for deletion via removeInstructionsAndOperands. The recursive deletion logic might get confused about which instruction is "trivially dead" first when there are circular dependencies through the uses in %c and %d."}

name: verify
arguments:
{"orig_ir": "```llvm
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-unknown"

define dso_local i32 @test() {
entry:
  %a = add i32 1, 2
  %b = add i32 3, 4
  
  ; Create a constant expression user (non-instruction)
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr null, i32 %a
  
  ; Store to array to enable vectorization
  %arr = alloca [2 x i32], align 8
  %gep0 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 0
  store i32 %a, ptr %gep0, align 4
  %gep1 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 1
  store i32 %b, ptr %gep1, align 4
  
  ret i32 0
}
```", "args": "-passes=slp-vectorizer", "thoughts": "Testing the "Non-instruction users" strategy for test 3. The test creates an add instruction %a that is used by a constant expression (getelementptr). When SLP vectorizes the stores, it should mark the add instructions for deletion via removeInstructionsAndOperands. The code assumes all users are instructions for deletion checks, but constant expressions are not instructions. This might cause issues when checking if the instruction has users or when determining if it's trivially dead."}

name: report
arguments:
{"test": ["```llvm
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-unknown"

define dso_local i32 @test() {
entry:
  %a = add i32 1, 2
  %b = add i32 3, 4
  
  ; Create a circular dependency: %a uses %b, %b uses %a
  %c = add i32 %a, %b
  %d = add i32 %b, %a
  
  ; Store to array to enable vectorization
  %arr = alloca [4 x i32], align 16
  %gep0 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 0
  store i32 %a, ptr %gep0, align 4
  %gep1 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 1
  store i32 %b, ptr %gep1, align 4
  %gep2 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 2
  store i32 %c, ptr %gep2, align 4
  %gep3 = getelementptr inbounds [4 x i32], ptr %arr, i64 0, i64 3
  store i32 %d, ptr %gep3, align 4
  
  ret i32 0
}
```", "opt -S -passes=slp-vectorizer"], "thoughts": "## Bug Triggering Analysis\n\nThe test case creates a circular dependency where %c uses %a and %b, and %d uses %b and %a. When SLP vectorizer processes the stores, it should mark %a and %b for deletion via `removeInstructionsAndOperands`. However, the recursive deletion logic in `removeInstructionsAndOperands` might get confused about which instruction is \"trivially dead\" first when there are circular dependencies through the uses in %c and %d.\n\nThe algorithm checks if operands have only one user and are trivially dead. With circular dependencies:\n1. %a has users: store, %c, %d (3 users) - not single user\n2. %b has users: store, %c, %d (3 users) - not single user\n3. %c has user: store (1 user) - might be marked as trivially dead\n4. %d has user: store (1 user) - might be marked as trivially dead\n\nBut %c and %d depend on %a and %b, creating a circular chain. The algorithm might incorrectly delete %c and %d first, then try to delete %a and %b, but they still have store users.\n\n## Fix Weakness Analysis\n\nThe fix introduces `removeInstructionsAndOperands` which recursively marks trivially dead operands for deletion. The weakness is in the circular dependency handling:\n\n1. The algorithm uses `wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead` and `isInstructionTriviallyDead` which might not properly handle circular dependencies.\n2. When nulling out operands (`OpU.set(nullptr)`), it checks if the operand becomes dead. With circular dependencies, this could create a situation where instructions reference each other through nulled operands.\n3. The algorithm processes dead instructions in a worklist, but circular dependencies could cause infinite loops or incorrect ordering.\n\nSpecifically, the check `OpI->hasOneUser()` might fail for circular dependencies because each instruction has multiple users in the cycle. However, after some instructions are marked for deletion, the user count might change, leading to inconsistent state.\n\nThe fix assumes a linear dependency chain, but circular dependencies violate this assumption."}

```",

  "thoughts": "## Bug Triggering Analysis\n\nThe test case creates a circular dependency where %c uses %a and %b, and %d uses %b and %a. When SLP vectorizer processes the stores, it should mark %a and %b for deletion via `removeInstructionsAndOperands`. However, the recursive deletion logic in `removeInstructionsAndOperands` might get confused about which instruction is \"trivially dead\" first when there are circular dependencies through the uses in %c and %d.\n\nThe algorithm checks if operands have only one user and are trivially dead. With circular dependencies:\n1. %a has users: store, %c, %d (3 users) - not single user\n2. %b has users: store, %c, %d (3 users) - not single user\n3. %c has user: store (1 user) - might be marked as trivially dead\n4. %d has user: store (1 user) - might be marked as trivially dead\n\nBut %c and %d depend on %a and %b, creating a circular chain. The algorithm might incorrectly delete %c and %d first, then try to delete %a and %b, but they still have store users.\n\n## Fix Weakness Analysis\n\nThe fix introduces `removeInstructionsAndOperands` which recursively marks trivially dead operands for deletion. The weakness is in the circular dependency handling:\n\n1. The algorithm uses `wouldInstructionBeTriviallyDead` and `isInstructionTriviallyDead` which might not properly handle circular dependencies.\n2. When nulling out operands (`OpU.set(nullptr)`), it checks if the operand becomes dead. With circular dependencies, this could create a situation where instructions reference each other through nulled operands.\n3. The algorithm processes dead instructions in a worklist, but circular dependencies could cause infinite loops or incorrect ordering.\n\nSpecifically, the check `OpI->hasOneUser()` might fail for circular dependencies because each instruction has multiple users in the cycle. However, after some instructions are marked for deletion, the user count might change, leading to inconsistent state.\n\nThe fix assumes a linear dependency chain, but circular dependencies violate this assumption."
